patterns.name: Skipping the Invisible Steps
patterns.what: Kaiyangnggg often understands the final outcome of an experiment, but he occasionally skips mentioning the invisible scientific processes (like 'gaining heat' or 'trapping air') that make it happen.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Kaiyangnggg correctly noted the chocolate expanded and burst, but missed the invisible step that it first 'gained heat' and expanded 'more than' the bun.
He knew the cup sleeve prevents scalding, but forgot to mention the hidden mechanism: that the corrugation 'traps air', which is a 'poor conductor of heat'.
He correctly stated that more heat energy is needed for two packets of fishballs, but skipped mentioning the reason: there is 'more mass' to heat up.

patterns.strategic_advice: When you see **'explain why'** or **'explain how'**, imagine you are telling a story in slow motion. Always include the **'invisible middle step'** (like gaining/losing heat, or air being a poor conductor) before stating the final consequence. Writing down a quick chain of events on the side can help!

patterns.name: Tracing Paths and Arrows
patterns.what: When dealing with diagrams that show movement, Kaiyangnggg sometimes gets the direction of the arrows mixed up, especially for light rays bouncing into our eyes or forces pushing against movement.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: When tracing how Peter sees the painting, Kaiyangnggg picked arrows pointing away from the eye instead of light bouncing off the painting and into the eye.
He found it a bit tricky to identify the correct arrows for frictional and gravitational forces, picking an arrow pointing in the same direction as the pull instead of opposite to it.
When drawing arrows to show how food is transported in a plant, he drew the arrow from the leaves to the fruits instead of from the stem.

patterns.strategic_advice: When a question asks about **'direction'** or shows **'arrows'**, trace the path with your finger first. For light, remember it always travels **from the source, bounces off the object, and into the eye**. For friction, the arrow must always point **opposite to the movement**.

patterns.name: Decoding Graphs and Shadows
patterns.what: Kaiyangnggg occasionally finds it a bit tricky to translate 2D graphs into real-world changes, or to match the size of a shadow to how close an object is to a light source.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: When looking at a heating curve graph, Kaiyangnggg missed that the flat line (melting) also represents the ice gaining heat.
He found it tricky to match the size of the shadows to the shapes, forgetting that the object closest to the torch casts the largest shadow.
When reading a graph about organism populations at different water depths, he occasionally finds it tricky to identify which organism thrives best with more light.

patterns.strategic_advice: When you see a **'graph'** or a **'shadow'** diagram, label the parts before looking at the options. For shadows, write **'closer = bigger'** next to the torch. For temperature graphs, label the flat lines as **'changing state (still gaining/losing heat)'**.

patterns.name: Mixing Up Scientific Vocabulary
patterns.what: Kaiyangnggg sometimes swaps his scientific vocabulary, such as confusing which materials are good conductors of heat, or mixing up different types of energy and forces.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Kaiyangnggg chose a glass container instead of a metal one when asked which would cool hot water the fastest, mixing up which material is the better heat conductor.
He correctly identified some energy changes but mixed up his vocabulary, writing 'potential energy' instead of 'kinetic energy' for the moving air.
When asked about the energy conversion of a switched-on phone, he confused the starting energy, picking 'electrical energy' instead of 'potential energy' stored in the battery.

patterns.strategic_advice: When a question asks about **'lowest temperature'** or **'energy conversion'**, pause and define the materials or energy first. Write **'metal = fast heat loss'** or **'movement = kinetic energy'** right on the paper to anchor your thoughts before choosing.
